text: The surgeon demonstrates the port positions for the robotic da Vinci SI system. The falciform ligament is divided using ultrasonic shears, extending the division up to the hepatic veins. Carriage dissection is initiated.
The surgeon uses scissors in the robotic arm one and cadier forceps in arm two, while retracting the falciform ligament with an endo loop.
The surgeon begins hilar dissection using Maryland bipolar forceps and monopolar curved scissors, carefully looping the right hepatic artery.
The surgeon dissects the right portal vein and applies a silk tape around it. The right hepatic artery and right portal vein are clipped using a hemoloclip applicator.

The surgeon divides the right portal vein, leading to visible color demarcation on the liver surface. Parenchymal surface marking is performed, and the robotic Firefly technology is utilized to clearly highlight the line of demarcation between liver segments.

The liver is retracted on both sides using a rubber band stitch. The console surgeon performs initial surface parenchymal transection with ultrasonic shears and Maryland bipolar forceps. The assistant surgeon uses CUSA for deeper parenchymal transection while also suctioning the surgical field.
With rubber band traction applied on either side of the liver, the surgeon achieves sufficient exposure to perform parenchymal transection, visibly opening the liver tissue.
The assistant surgeon applies the QSA while identifying the Segment V vein draining into the middle hepatic vein. The vein is then clipped and divided.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis using a combination of bipolar and ultrasonic shears. The hilar plate is dissected and divided intraparenchymally. The robotic firefly system with ICG is utilized to visually confirm the bile duct division.
The surgeon loops and divides the large inferior right hepatic vein using an endo-GIA stapler. The segment eight vein is isolated, clipped with hemolog and metal clips, and then divided.
The surgeon divides the right hepatic vein using an endo-GIA stapler, completing the transection of this major vessel.

The surgeon releases all lateral attachments to the right lobe of the liver, including diaphragmatic attachments of the tumor to the posterior superior aspect. Robotic instruments provide stable traction, facilitating dissection in challenging areas. The right lobe is lifted from the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon divides the hepatocaval ligament, also known as Makuchi's ligament, using an endo-GIA stapler, completing the parenchymal transection.
